Dear Dr Ahmed,

Liam O’Connor, DOB: 12-05-1987, Hospital No: M938417 NHS No. 8094523176  
22 Fern Close, Manchester, M4 2LP

Diagnosis: Generalised epilepsy.

Medication:
- Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily.
- Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily.

Clinical summary:
Since optimising the above regimen, there has been clear ongoing progress. The patient reports that day-to-day functioning has stabilised and, importantly, there have been effectively no spell-like events suggestive of seizures over the past six months. This represents a marked improvement on current medicines compared with earlier periods. For context, prior to this improvement, his first seizure occurred in November 2014 in Ireland, at night while asleep, with waking to rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a prominent sense of déjà vu. The second event was in June 2015 in Scotland, again during sleep, lasting about five minutes with a similar semiology.

Current status and review:
- No witnessed convulsions, no episodes of loss of awareness, and no nocturnal jerks reported by bed partner since the last visit.  
- He notes steadier mood and better sleep quality after adhering to a regular bedtime routine and avoiding missed doses.  
- No adverse effects from medication beyond mild transient morning nausea in the first fortnight after dose adjustment, now resolved.  

Lifestyle and counselling:
- We discussed ongoing adherence, sleep hygiene, and avoidance of alcohol binges, which he is managing well.  
- He does not drive and understands the DVLA guidance relating to epilepsy; he will inform us immediately should any events recur.  

Plan:
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily without change.  
- Routine bloods (FBC, LFTs, U&Es) at his GP surgery in three months as part of standard monitoring.  
- We will review in six months, or sooner if there is any return of seizure-like activity.

Yours sincerely,

Dear Dr Patel,

Daniela Morris, DOB: 23-04-1991, Hospital No: H482930 NHS No. 9384756210  
12 Bramble Court, London, W2 6JP

Medication:

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Rescue medication: Buccal midazolam 10 mg for convulsive seizures lasting longer than 5 minutes (not used in this interval).

Diagnosis and summary:

This patient has Combined Generalised and Focal Epilepsy, with a history of both generalised tonic–clonic seizures and focal impaired-awareness seizures. Over the interval since the last review, her seizure diary indicates that there have been no recorded events, either focal or generalised, during the period monitored.

History since last visit:

She continues to use a paper seizure calendar, which she brings to each appointment. The calendar entries from 01 June 2025 to 30 September 2025 document consistent daily logs without gaps. During this timeframe she notes no auras, no brief staring spells, and no convulsive episodes. Sleep patterns have been stable and she reports adherence to medication without missed doses. She has not identified any new triggers, though she continues to avoid sleep deprivation and excess alcohol. Family members have not witnessed any nocturnal events. She has been attending work full-time and resumed regular exercise without difficulty.

Examination/investigations:

Neurological examination today is unremarkable. No adverse effects attributable to medication beyond occasional transient dizziness in the mornings, which she associates with taking both medications together; she prefers to continue this schedule for simplicity. Recent bloods (U&E, LFTs) from her GP in September were within normal limits. No new imaging or EEG requested at this stage given the stable course.

Plan:

- Continue levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily and lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily unchanged.  
- Continue to maintain the paper seizure calendar; she will bring logs to the next review.  
- Safety: she remains aware of SUDEP and seizure first aid; rescue midazolam remains available but has not been required.  
- Driving: she does not currently drive and understands DVLA guidance should this change.  
- Follow-up in 6 months, or earlier if there is any recurrence of focal symptoms (such as rising epigastric sensations, speech disturbance, or automatisms) or any generalised tonic–clonic seizure.

Yours sincerely

Communication was supported by a pre-arranged speakerphone connection with his sister present to assist, in line with his hearing difficulties and expressed preference. He reports that since titration to levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily in August 2023, there have been no further events suggestive of seizures. He has avoided alcohol binges and high sleep debt, which he previously identified as potential triggers.

Given the sustained period without episodes and good tolerability, I have advised continuation of current therapy and routine safety measures. We discussed the importance of contacting our service promptly should there be any breakthrough events, medication issues, or concerns raised by family observers. 

Follow-up will be arranged as a scheduled speakerphone consultation at 12 months with his sister assisting communication, in keeping with his preference. No routine face-to-face appointment is required at present; however, I remain happy to see him sooner if concerns arise.

She reports improved energy levels compared to earlier in the year and no new intercurrent illnesses. There is no current concern regarding mood or sleep beyond occasional pre-examination anxiety, which she manages with standard breathing techniques taught by our epilepsy nurse specialist.

Regarding recent events: In February she experienced a prolonged focal seizure lasting 8 minutes, resolving without intervention. In May there were four further brief absences, and in August a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure was reported at school (not witnessed by family). These episodes have been clearly recorded in her diary, with triggers likely related to missed meals and sleep deprivation around coursework deadlines. Since adopting structured reminders in late August, she has had no further events and has taken all doses on time.

Neurological examination today is unremarkable. There are no drug-related rashes, no tremor, and no cognitive concerns on bedside screening. We discussed safety and rescue planning; no rescue medication has been required. She prefers to avoid changes while her control is improving, and I agree that stability is prudent given the recent positive trajectory.

Plan:
- Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. unchanged.
- Maintain seizure diary and phone reminders; continue avoidance of sleep deprivation and missed meals.
- Bloods today for routine monitoring (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) – to be copied to you.
- Review in clinic in 4 months, sooner if breakthrough seizures recur or if tolerability issues arise.

He has therefore moved the device to a more accessible outer pocket and informed supervisors about his condition and the location of first‑aid stations. Examination: Weight 86 kg (BMI 27.3). Pulse 68 bpm regular, BP 124/76 mmHg. Neurological examination grossly normal; gait steady; no focal deficits. Plan:
